context: B2B SaaS with 25 new customers/month, €100k total sales+marketing spend, €300/month ARPU, 80% gross margin.
diagnosis: Mediocre LTV/CAC with dangerous payback. The business works but barely. Needs pricing increase or sales cycle optimization.
applicability: B2B SaaS with moderate ARPU and sales-assisted motion

context: Enterprise software selling to banks. CAC €12,000 per customer. ARPU €5,000/month. 85% gross margin. Average retention: 4 years.
diagnosis: Extremely healthy unit economics despite high CAC. Payback under 3 months. LTV/CAC > 5 suggests possible under-investment in growth. Should aggressively scale enterprise sales team.
applicability: Enterprise B2B with high ACV and long retention

context: SMB SaaS with self-serve signup. CAC €800 (mostly paid ads). ARPU €49/month. 75% gross margin. Monthly churn: 8%.
diagnosis: DEAD. LTV/CAC below 1.0. Every customer acquired is a net loss. The 8% monthly churn means average customer life is only 12.5 months — they churn before ever repaying CAC. Immediate freeze on paid acquisition. Fix retention before spending another euro on ads.
applicability: SMB SaaS with high churn and low ARPU

context: Fintech startup preparing Series A. Blended CAC reported as €1,500 but 60% of customers come organically. Paid-only CAC is €3,750. ARPU €200/month. 70% gross margin.
diagnosis: Classic self-deception. Blended CAC looks healthy (3.36x) but paid-only CAC is unsustainable (26.8 months payback). If organic growth slows — which it always does — the real economics are mediocre. Must present both numbers honestly to investors.
applicability: Startup mixing organic and paid acquisition metrics
